claims.claim: Cards must maintain a thoughtful information hierarchy to be skimmable; without it they fail as a UI pattern.
claims.quote: They're skimmable sometimes actionable but in order to be successful, need to have a thoughtful hierarchy.

claims.claim: A card's core function is surfacing content and actions for a single piece of information while eliminating irrelevant noise.
claims.quote: Cards are typically used to surface content and actions for a single piece of information with the goal of giving relevant data and removing all of the noise.

claims.claim: Card components derive their perceived coherence from Gestalt principles — individual elements (image, badge, text, button) are perceived as a unified whole rather than discrete parts.
claims.quote: This goes back to the Gestalt theory that the whole is greater than the sum of its parts when discussing how individual card elements combine into a cohesive design.

claims.claim: Card text must meet a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio against the background color to satisfy web accessibility standards.
claims.quote: Your text needs to have enough contrast from the background color to be able to pass a 4.5:1 ratio.

claims.claim: Color carries semantic load in card status indicators — green signals success and red signals failure in western-cultural interfaces, making color choice non-arbitrary for category badges.
claims.quote: Green intrinsically means success, while red denotes failure in most western cultures.
